disease.name: Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm (AAA)
disease.note: Permanent dilation of the abdominal aorta >3cm. Risk of rupture increases exponentially with size. In Kenya, AAA is underdiagnosed due to limited screening — most present as emergencies with rupture. Smoking is the single most important modifiable risk factor. Elective repair (open or EVAR) for aneurysms >5.5cm or rapid expansion carries 1-5% mortality vs 80%+ for emergency repair of rupture. Screening ultrasound in at-risk populations (men >65, smokers, family history) saves lives.
presentingComplaints: Deep abdominal or back pain (warning sign)
Sudden severe abdominal or back pain with collapse
Feeling faint or dizzy with abdominal pain
Abdominal pain radiating to the back or groin

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Ruptured/symptomatic/tender/large >5.5cm/rapid expansion -> SEVERE; small asymptomatic (<5.5cm, stable) -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: Was this aneurysm found incidentally or because of symptoms?
Do you have any abdominal or back pain?
Did the pain start suddenly?
Do you feel a pulsating mass in your abdomen?
Have you felt faint, dizzy, or lost consciousness?
Do you smoke tobacco or use khat/miraa?
How many pack-years of smoking? (packs per day × years)
Do you have high blood pressure?
Do you have any family history of aortic aneurysm?
Do you have COPD or emphysema?
How old are you?
Do you have any leg pain when walking (claudication)?
Do you have diabetes, high cholesterol, or heart disease?
Have you ever had a heart attack or stroke?
Do you have any other aneurysms (popliteal, femoral)?
Have you had any previous abdominal surgeries?
Do you take any blood thinners (aspirin, warfarin, clopidogrel)?
What is your kidney function like — any known kidney disease?
Do you have any allergies to contrast dye?
Are you able to climb two flights of stairs? (fitness assessment)
Have you ever had a CT or ultrasound scan of your abdomen before?
Do you have any connective tissue disease (Marfan, Ehlers-Danlos, Loeys-Dietz)?
Do you have a bicuspid aortic valve (known)?
Do you have any other aneurysms elsewhere (popliteal, femoral, thoracic)?
Have you had any previous vascular surgeries?
What is your exercise capacity — can you walk up a hill or two flights of stairs without stopping?
Do you have any chest pain, hoarseness, or difficulty swallowing (suggests thoracic aneurysm)?
Have you had any recent unexplained weight loss or fever (inflammatory AAA)?
Do you have any leg swelling, DVT, or pulmonary embolism history?
Do you have any known carotid artery disease or previous stroke/TIA?
What medications are you currently taking — including over-the-counter?
Do you have any allergies to medications, latex, or iodine?
Have you ever had radiation therapy to your abdomen or pelvis?
Do you drink alcohol — how many units per week?
Do you have any chronic steroid or immunosuppressant use?
Have you had any recent infections or dental procedures (risk of graft infection)?
Do you have any kidney stones or chronic kidney disease?
What is your ethnicity — AAA is more common in Caucasian populations but affects Africans too
Do you have any back surgery or spinal instrumentation?
Have you discussed AAA screening with your GP or had a screening ultrasound?
Are you sexually active — erectile dysfunction can be related to iliac aneurysm or surgery risk?
Do you understand the risks of elective repair vs surveillance for your aneurysm size?
Who is your next of kin — we need to discuss advance care planning for a major surgery
Do you have a living will or advance directive?

historyFeatures.note: Warning sign of expansion or rupture

examination_findings.finding: Palpable pulsatile abdominal mass (midline, above umbilicus)
Abdominal tenderness over the mass (suggests expansion or rupture)
Hypotension and tachycardia (ruptured AAA)
Abdominal bruit on auscultation
Abdominal distension and rigidity (rupture with peritonitis)
Ecchymosis on flank (Gray Turner's sign) or periumbilical (Cullen's) — retroperitoneal bleed
Femoral pulse deficit or asymmetry
Distal embolization signs: blue toe, livedo reticularis
Signs of shock: cold clammy skin, delayed capillary refill, altered mental status
Popliteal or femoral aneurysm on palpation (associated aneurysms)
Diminished lower limb pulses
Abdominal tenderness on deep palpation
Palpable popliteal or femoral aneurysm on bilateral exam (10-20% have associated aneurysms)
Abdominal auscultation for bruit — suggests associated aortoiliac stenosis
Lower limb neurological exam — assess for peripheral neuropathy (associated with AAA)
Peripheral pulse examination: femoral, popliteal, dorsalis pedis, posterior tibial — document all
Check for inguinal hernia — associated with AAA (collagen weakness)
Skin exam for xanthelasma or tendon xanthomata (familial hypercholesterolemia risk)
Respiratory exam — assess for COPD (major risk factor and affects surgical candidacy)
Cardiac exam: JVP, heart sounds, murmurs, displaced apex (assess cardiac function)
Body habitus assessment: obesity makes palpation difficult; pannus may complicate wound healing
Measure blood pressure in both arms — difference suggests subclavian stenosis or dissection
Check for supraclavicular or carotid bruits — associated atherosclerotic disease
Fundoscopy for hypertensive retinopathy — assess chronic hypertension severity
Sacral and peripheral edema — assess for heart failure as comorbidity

bedside_scores.name: AAA Rupture Risk Score (Size-Based)
bedside_scores.interpretation: Score 1-2: surveillance; Score 3: consider repair if fit; Score 4-5: strong indication for elective repair. Rupture risk also increased by: female sex ×4, smoking ×2, hypertension ×1.5, COPD ×1.5, family history ×1.8

riskFactors.note: Less common in African populations but occurs, often with aggressive course

subtypes.name: Asymptomatic AAA (Screening/Surveillance)
subtypes.management: Surveillance: US every 3 years (3-4cm), every 6-12 months (4-5.4cm). Medical management: smoking cessation, statin (Atorvastatin 80mg daily), antiplatelet (Aspirin 75mg), BP control (target <130/80). Elective repair if >5.5cm or >0.5cm growth in 6 months or >1cm in 1 year.

subtypes.name: Symptomatic/Ruptured AAA
subtypes.management: Emergency repair. Open repair: cross-clamp infrarenal aorta, open sac, interposition graft (tubular or bifurcated Dacron graft). EVAR: if anatomically suitable (neck >15mm, angle <60°, iliac access adequate). Permissive hypotension (SBP 70-90 mmHg) until aortic control. Massive transfusion protocol (1:1:1 RBC:FFP:Platelets). ICU post-op.

bedside.name: Abdominal palpation for pulsatile mass
bedside.indication: Screening — sensitivity limited in obese patients

bedside.name: Urinary catheter
bedside.indication: Monitor urine output — essential for ruptured AAA

laboratory.name: CBC
laboratory.indication: Assess blood loss — Hb drop suggests rupture

laboratory.name: Blood Group and Crossmatch
laboratory.indication: Prepare for massive transfusion — order 6 units PRBC

laboratory.name: Creatinine, eGFR
laboratory.indication: Renal function — contrast risk for CTA, surgical planning

laboratory.name: Coagulation profile (PT, aPTT, INR)
laboratory.indication: DIC can occur with rupture, baseline before surgery

laboratory.name: Lactate
laboratory.indication: Tissue hypoperfusion marker — elevated in rupture

laboratory.name: Troponin
laboratory.indication: Assess cardiac strain or concomitant MI

imaging.name: Abdominal Ultrasound
imaging.indication: Screening and surveillance — accurate size measurement

imaging.name: CT Aorta with IV contrast (CT Angiography)
imaging.indication: Gold standard for pre-op planning — size, morphology, neck length, iliac anatomy, rupture assessment

imaging.name: CT Abdomen without contrast
imaging.indication: If ruptured and unstable — limited study to confirm rupture, saves time

imaging.name: Plain X-Ray Abdomen (AP + lateral)
imaging.indication: Calcified aortic wall outline may be visible — incidental finding

imaging.name: Echocardiogram (TTE/TEE)
imaging.indication: Pre-op cardiac risk assessment in elective cases

imaging.name: Carotid Duplex
imaging.indication: Screen for concomitant carotid stenosis in elective cases

resuscitation: For ruptured AAA: permissive hypotension (SBP 70-90 mmHg) — avoid aggressive fluid resuscitation which increases bleeding
Limited volume resuscitation with blood products if tolerated
Immediate transfer to operating theatre or hybrid suite
Cross-match 6-10 units PRBC, FFP, Platelets, Cryoprecipitate
conservative: For small asymptomatic AAA: surveillance program
Medical optimization: smoking cessation (most important), statin therapy (Atorvastatin 80mg), antiplatelet (Aspirin 75mg), BP control (target <130/80 mmHg)
Exercise: moderate activity is safe — no evidence that heavy lifting causes rupture

surgical: Open surgical repair (OSR): midline laparotomy, cross-clamp infrarenal aorta, open aneurysm sac, interposition graft (Dacron tubular or bifurcated)
Endovascular aneurysm repair (EVAR): percutaneous or open femoral access, stent-graft deployment under fluoroscopy — shorter recovery, lower peri-op morbidity, similar long-term outcomes
Open repair preferred for: young patients, unsuitable anatomy for EVAR, connective tissue disease
nonOperative: Surveillance for small AAA (3-5.4cm) — serial US
Medical management only if unfit for any repair

general: Monitor: BP, HR, urine output, Hb, lactate, ABG
Check distal pulses — ankle brachial index
open_repair: NG tube until bowel function returns
Monitor for: abdominal compartment syndrome, colonic ischemia (bloody diarrhea, rising lactate), renal failure, cardiac complications
IV fluids until oral intake adequate
evar: Bed rest for 4-6h if percutaneous (closure device)
Monitor access site for hematoma, pseudoaneurysm
CT angiogram within 30 days (post-EVAR surveillance: at 1, 6, 12 months, then annual)
Lifelong surveillance: CTA or Duplex at 1, 6, 12 months, then annually
day1_3: Pain control: epidural (open) or IV analgesia
day3_7: Discharge planning: antihypertensives, statin, antiplatelet, smoking cessation

pathways.treatment: Surveillance US: 3-4cm every 3 years; 4-5.4cm every 6-12 months. Medical: statin, antiplatelet, BP control, smoking cessation.

pathways.criteria: size >=5.5cm or expansion >1cm/year or >0.5cm/6 months or symptomatic
pathways.treatment: Elective repair. EVAR if suitable anatomy (neck >15mm, angle <60°, iliac ≥7mm). Open repair if unfit for EVAR or complex anatomy (short neck, angulated, iliac occlusive disease).

pathways.criteria: hypotension, abdominal/back pain, known AAA, CT confirms rupture
pathways.treatment: Emergency repair (open or EVAR depending on anatomy and center expertise). Permissive hypotension. Massive transfusion. ICU.

operative_templates.procedure: Open Infrarenal AAA Repair (Tube or Bifurcated Graft)
operative_templates.indication: AAA >5.5cm or rapid expansion or symptomatic/ruptured — unsuitable for EVAR
operative_templates.position: Supine, arms tucked, nasogastric tube and urinary catheter
operative_templates.incision: Midline laparotomy from xiphisternum to pubis
steps: Full midline laparotomy
Retract omentum and transverse colon superiority
Incise posterior peritoneum over aorta from Treitz ligament to iliac bifurcation
Identify and protect left renal vein crossing aorta
Dissect infrarenal aortic neck circumferentially — place aortic clamp
Dissect common iliac arteries — place vessel loops
Heparin 5,000 IU IV given before clamping
Clamp aorta (infrarenal) and both common iliac arteries
Open aneurysm sac longitudinally
Evacuate thrombus and atheroma
Oversew lumbar arteries from inside sac with 2-0 silk
Oversew inferior mesenteric artery if patent
Select appropriate graft (tube vs bifurcated Dacron)
Proximal anastomosis: 3-0 Prolene, continuous, full-thickness aortic wall at neck
Distal anastomosis: to aortic bifurcation (tube) or to common iliac arteries (bifurcated) with 3-0 or 4-0 Prolene
Check for leaks before closing sac
Flush and declamp sequentially
Close aneurysm sac over graft ('sac wrap')
Close posterior peritoneum over graft
Check distal pulses — Doppler signal in feet
keyLandmarks: Left renal vein — crossing aorta, must protect or ligate if needed (rarely)
Infrarenal aortic neck — clamp site, must be free of thrombus and calcification
Inferior mesenteric artery — oversew and assess colonic viability
Iliac bifurcation — distal clamp and anastomosis site
Ureters — cross iliac bifurcation, protect throughout

operative_templates.indication: AAA >5.5cm with suitable anatomy for endovascular repair
operative_templates.approach: Percutaneous common femoral artery access (Preclose technique) or bilateral groin cutdown
operative_templates.incision: Small (1-2cm) incisions over both groins for femoral artery exposure — or bilateral percutaneous access
steps: Bilateral common femoral artery access — retrograde with micropuncture needle
Place 6F sheaths initially
Aortogram to confirm neck length, diameter, and renal artery origins
Advance stiff guidewires (Lunderquist) into thoracic aorta
Upgrade to large-bore sheaths (14-22F) depending on device
Deploy main body stent-graft below lowest renal artery
Contralateral gate cannulation from contralateral access
Deploy contralateral iliac limb
Deploy ipsilateral iliac limb
Completion angiogram: confirm exclusion of aneurysm sac, no endoleak (type I or III), patent renal and iliac arteries
Balloon-mold proximal and distal seal zones
Close arteriotomies (Proglide/xPert closure devices or direct suture)
keyLandmarks: Lowest renal artery — proximal landing zone, must preserve flow
Aortic bifurcation — contralateral gate opens above this
Internal iliac arteries — preserve at least one to prevent buttock claudication and colon ischemia
External iliac arteries — distal landing zone, must be ≥7mm diameter

watchFor: decreased seal zone on surveillance imaging

dischargeCriteria: Hemodynamically stable — normotensive without IV vasopressors
No signs of bleeding or anemia requiring transfusion
Abdominal pain controlled on oral analgesia
Bowel function returned — passing flatus, tolerating diet
Creatinine stable or returning to baseline
Distal pulses present in both feet (or Doppler-audible)
CT/MRA done within 30 days (for EVAR) as baseline

patient_education.explanation: An abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) is a widening of the main blood vessel in your belly (the aorta). Think of it like a balloon that slowly inflates — if it gets too big, it can burst (rupture), which is life-threatening. We monitor small aneurysms with regular ultrasound. When it reaches a certain size (5.5cm) or grows quickly, we recommend repair — either with open surgery (graft replacement) or with a stent-graft (EVAR) through the groin arteries. The risk of rupture without repair is higher than the risk of elective surgery.
warningSigns: Sudden severe abdominal or back pain (rupture warning)
Pain radiating to the groin or leg
Fever or wound redness after surgery
activity: After open repair: no heavy lifting >5kg for 6-8 weeks
After EVAR: no heavy lifting for 2 weeks
Gentle walking is encouraged from day 1 after surgery
Sexual activity: can resume when comfortable, usually 4-6 weeks
diet: Heart-healthy diet: low salt, low saturated fat, plenty of fruits and vegetables
If constipated: avoid straining — use stool softeners temporarily after surgery
medication: Aspirin 75mg daily for life (prevents graft thrombosis and cardiovascular events)
Blood pressure medications as prescribed — keep BP <130/80
Smoking cessation is the most important thing you can do
If on blood thinners after EVAR: take as directed
followUp: After EVAR: CT scan at 1 month, 6 months, 12 months, then annually for life
After open repair: clinical review + US/CT at 1 year, then every 2-3 years
Annual vascular check for life even after successful repair
Monitor for new aneurysms elsewhere (popliteal, femoral, thoracic)

before_discharge: Medication counseling: statin, antiplatelet, antihypertensive compliance
Wound care — groin and/or midline incision care
Smoking cessation — strong emphasis, offer referral
Surveillance imaging schedule discussed (especially for EVAR)

after_discharge.action: Clinical review — assess recovery, BP control, smoking status
Repeat CT/CTA if EVAR — assess for endoleak, sac size
Annual CT/CTA (EVAR) or US (open repair) — lifelong surveillance
Cardiac risk assessment — ECG, troponin, stress test if indicated
Consider screening for thoracic aortic aneurysm if bicuspid valve or family history

when_to_return: Sudden severe abdominal or back pain
Chest pain or shortness of breath
Leg pain, coldness, or color change
Black or bloody stools (possible aortoenteric fistula)

pathophysiologyDetail: AAA is not atherosclerosis — it is a distinct degenerative process: chronic transmural inflammation (macrophages, CD4+ T cells, B cells) → elastin fragmentation by matrix metalloproteinases (MMP-2, MMP-9) → loss of medial smooth muscle cells by apoptosis → compensatory collagen deposition → wall weakening → progressive dilation

Laplace's law governs aneurysm expansion: wall tension (T) = pressure (P) × radius (r). As radius increases, wall tension increases exponentially — creating a positive feedback loop where larger aneurysms expand faster and have higher rupture risk

Intraluminal thrombus is not protective — it is a biologically active structure: activated platelets within the thrombus release IL-6, TNF-α, and MMP-9; the thrombus creates focal wall hypoxia (reduced oxygen diffusion by 90%) → localized wall weakening; thrombus thickness >10 mm is associated with faster expansion

Oxidative stress: superoxide production by NADPH oxidase (NOX1, NOX4) in aneurysm walls is 5-10x higher than in normal aorta. Reactive oxygen species activate MMPs, promote smooth muscle cell apoptosis, and induce endothelial dysfunction

Genetic basis: 15-20% of AAAs are familial. Key loci: 4q31 (PCSK5 — linked to TGF-β signaling), 19q13 (LRP1 — LDL receptor-related protein). Syndromic: FBN1 (Marfan), COL3A1 (Ehlers-Danlos type IV), TGFBR1/TGFBR2 (Loeys-Dietz), ACTA2 (familial thoracic aortic aneurysm)

Smoking is the strongest modifiable risk factor: smokers have 4-6x higher risk of AAA. Smoking increases MMP activity, promotes oxidative stress, and accelerates elastin degradation. Cessation reduces expansion rate by 30-40%

In Kenya, AAA is less prevalent than in Western populations (age-standardized prevalence ~1-2% vs 4-8% in Caucasian men over 65), but it is underdiagnosed because of lack of screening programs. Most AAAs present with rupture (70%) rather than as incidental findings on screening ultrasound

Ruptured AAA physiology: when wall stress exceeds wall strength, the posterior or posterolateral wall (weakest point) ruptures → retroperitoneal hemorrhage → tamponade (temporary) → hypotension → decompensation → free intraperitoneal rupture → exsanguination and death unless emergency repair is performed within 2 hours

Inflammatory AAA: a variant accounting for 5-10% of AAAs, characterized by thick-walled, perianeurysmal fibrosis with dense lymphoplasmacytic infiltration. Presents with back pain, weight loss, elevated ESR/CRP. Has a distinct pathophysiology involving IgG4-related disease in some cases

EVAR-specific pathophysiology: after stent-graft placement, the aneurysm sac is excluded from the circulation. Successful EVAR induces sac shrinkage (thrombus organization → fibrosis → sac contraction). Failure occurs via endoleak (Types I-V): Type I (attachment site leak) pressurizes the sac → expansion → rupture risk. Type II (retrograde flow from lumbar/IMA) causes 30-40% spontaneous thrombosis but 10-20% require reintervention. Type III (fabric tear or junctional separation) is a structural failure requiring urgent relining. The sac behavior post-EVAR is the most important predictor of long-term success

Rupture risk is not linear — it increases exponentially with diameter: from <1% per year at 4.0 cm (similar to background) to 5-10% per year at 5.5 cm, 15-20% at 6.5 cm, and >30% at 7.5 cm. This non-linear relationship is governed by the physics of wall stress: Laplace's law (T = PR) underestimates stress because AAA shape is not a perfect sphere. Finite element analysis shows peak wall stress occurs at the posterior wall (the 'rupture-prone' posterolateral region) and at sites of asymmetric thrombus

Biomechanics of rupture: aortic wall strength is determined by collagen content and cross-linking. In AAA, the ratio of collagen III to collagen I increases (weaker collagen), and non-enzymatic glycation (AGE cross-links) reduces collagen flexibility. Wall stress exceeds wall strength when the aneurysm reaches a 'critical stress threshold' — typically at 5-6 cm in men, 4.5-5 cm in women (women have smaller aortas but thinner walls). Wall stress of >200 kPa predicts imminent rupture with 80% accuracy

The role of the intraluminal thrombus (ILT): ILT is not inert — it is a metabolically active structure that traps platelets, red cells, and leukocytes. The luminal surface of the ILT is highly thrombogenic (tissue factor expression). Over time, the ILT causes focal hypoxia of the aortic wall (oxygen diffusion reduced by 70-90%) → increased MMP-9 and MMP-2 activity at the ILT-arterial wall interface → focal wall weakening. Paradoxically, a thick ILT provides a 'cushion' against wall stress but accelerates biological degradation of the wall — the overall effect depends on ILT composition and patient factors

Familial AAA genetics: 15-20% of AAA patients have a first-degree relative with AAA. Screening of siblings of AAA patients (men >50, women >55) finds AAA in 20-30%. Multiple susceptibility loci identified: 9p21 (CDKN2B-AS1 — ANRIL, also associated with CAD), 19q13 (LRP1), 4q31 (PCSK5), 3p12 (FGF-binding protein 1). These loci implicate pathways of TGF-β signaling, lipid metabolism, and extracellular matrix remodeling. Genetic testing is not yet clinical routine but risk stratification calculators exist

In Kenya, AAA has unique features: (1) lower prevalence than in Caucasians but presents more aggressively — possibly due to untreated hypertension and smoking; (2) younger age at presentation (55-65 vs 65-75 in Western populations) — partly due to higher rates of connective tissue disorders (Marfan, Ehlers-Danlos) not diagnosed early; (3) higher proportion of inflammatory AAA (10-15% vs 5-10% in Western) — possibly related to endemic infections (TB, schistosomiasis, HIV) causing chronic inflammation that triggers or accelerates aneurysm formation; (4) ruptured AAA in Kenya has extremely high mortality (likely >80%) due to lack of pre-hospital EMS, limited blood transfusion capacity, and few centers capable of vascular repair

landmarkTrials.population: 1,090 patients with asymptomatic AAA 4.0-5.5 cm randomized to early elective surgery vs surveillance
landmarkTrials.finding: No survival benefit of early surgery (HR 0.94, p=0.56). Annual rupture risk for AAA <5.5 cm: 0.5-1%. Surveillance with threshold at 5.5 cm is safe
landmarkTrials.impact: Established the 5.5 cm threshold for elective AAA repair. Surveillance is the standard of care for AAA <5.5 cm
landmarkTrials.reference: UK Small Aneurysm Trial Participants. Lancet. 1998;352(9141):1649-1655

landmarkTrials.population: 1,136 VA patients with AAA 4.0-5.4 cm randomized to immediate repair (open or EVAR) vs surveillance
landmarkTrials.finding: No survival advantage for immediate repair. 5-year survival: 73% in both groups. AAA-related mortality was similar
landmarkTrials.impact: Reinforced 5.5 cm threshold from UKSAT. Together these two trials established level 1 evidence for surveillance of small AAAs
landmarkTrials.reference: Lederle FA, et al. N Engl J Med. 2002;346(19):1441-1448

landmarkTrials.population: 1,082 patients with AAA >5.5 cm fit for open repair randomized to EVAR vs open surgical repair (OSR)
landmarkTrials.finding: 30-day operative mortality: 1.7% for EVAR vs 4.7% for OSR (p<0.001). Aneurysm-related mortality at 4 years: 4% vs 7% (p=0.02). But: total mortality similar (28% vs 26%) due to non-AAA deaths
landmarkTrials.impact: EVAR has lower perioperative mortality than OSR but equivalent long-term survival. EVAR requires lifelong surveillance due to 20-30% endoleak/reintervention rate
landmarkTrials.reference: Greenhalgh RM, et al. EVAR trial 1. Lancet. 2005;365(9478):2179-2186

landmarkTrials.population: 351 patients with AAA >5.0 cm randomized to EVAR vs OSR in the Netherlands and Belgium
landmarkTrials.finding: 30-day mortality: 1.2% EVAR vs 4.6% OSR (p=0.10). Severe complications at 30 days: 12% vs 23% (p=0.02). 2-year survival similar (89% vs 90%)
landmarkTrials.impact: EVAR has reduced early morbidity but no long-term survival advantage over OSR. Patient preference and anatomy should guide choice
landmarkTrials.reference: Prinssen M, et al. DREAM Trial. N Engl J Med. 2005;353(18):1898-1904

landmarkTrials.population: 613 patients with clinical diagnosis of ruptured AAA randomized to EVAR-first vs open repair
landmarkTrials.finding: 30-day mortality: 35.4% EVAR vs 37.4% open repair (p=0.62). No significant difference in any outcome. EVAR strategy had cost-effectiveness advantage
landmarkTrials.impact: In ruptured AAA, EVAR and open repair have similar outcomes. The choice should depend on center expertise, patient anatomy, and hemodynamic stability

landmarkTrials.trial: MASS Trial — Multicentre Aneurysm Screening Study
landmarkTrials.population: 67,800 men aged 65-74 randomized to ultrasound screening vs no screening for AAA
landmarkTrials.finding: AAA-related mortality reduction: 42% (HR 0.58, p<0.001) in the screening group. Number needed to screen to prevent 1 death: 278. 10-year results confirmed sustained benefit
landmarkTrials.impact: Established national AAA screening programs (UK, Sweden, US). Screening is cost-effective and reduces AAA mortality by 50%
landmarkTrials.reference: Thompson SG, et al. MASS Trial. Lancet. 2007;369(9572):1531-1539

landmarkTrials.trial: OVER Trial (Open vs Endovascular Repair for AAA — VA Cooperative Study)
landmarkTrials.population: 881 patients with AAA >5.0 cm at 42 VA medical centers randomized to EVAR vs open surgical repair
landmarkTrials.finding: 30-day mortality: 0.5% EVAR vs 3.0% open (p=0.006). 2-year mortality: 7.0% vs 9.8% (p=0.12). Reintervention rate: 22% EVAR vs 18% open at 5 years (p=0.08). No difference in total mortality at 9 years. Endoleak rate: 14% at 1 year for EVAR
landmarkTrials.impact: Confirmed EVAR's early survival advantage disappears over time due to non-AAA deaths. Reintervention rates between EVAR and open repair converge at 5-8 years. Combined with EVAR-1 and DREAM, established equipoise between EVAR and open for long-term outcomes
landmarkTrials.reference: Lederle FA, et al. OVER Trial. N Engl J Med. 2012;367(21):1988-1997; N Engl J Med. 2019;380(22):2101-2110 (9-year results)

landmarkTrials.trial: EVAR-2 Trial (EVAR in Patients Unfit for Open Repair)
landmarkTrials.population: 338 patients with AAA >5.5 cm deemed unfit for open repair randomized to EVAR vs no intervention
landmarkTrials.finding: 30-day mortality: 9% EVAR (higher than expected in this high-risk population). Aneurysm-related death: 1.4 per 100 person-years (EVAR) vs 1.5 (no intervention, p=0.95). Total mortality: 64% in both groups at 4 years (most died of cardiovascular causes, not AAA). No survival benefit from EVAR in patients unfit for open surgery
landmarkTrials.impact: Controversial but crucial: EVAR does NOT improve survival in patients who are unfit for open surgery because they die of their comorbidities, not their AAA. Refined the selection criteria — 'fit for EVAR but not open' is NOT an indication if the patient has limited life expectancy from comorbidities. Decision-making must consider the patient's overall health, not just aneurysm anatomy
landmarkTrials.reference: Greenhalgh RM, et al. EVAR Trial 2. Lancet. 2005;365(9478):2187-2192

landmarkTrials.trial: PIVOTAL Trial (Positive Impact of Endovascular Options for Treating Aneurysms — Medtronic Device)
landmarkTrials.population: 1,452 patients with AAA >5.0 cm treated with the Endurant stent graft — a large device-specific registry with 5-year follow-up
landmarkTrials.finding: 5-year freedom from all-cause mortality: 68.5%. Freedom from aneurysm-related mortality: 95.3%. Freedom from reintervention: 77.4%. Type I/III endoleak at 5 years: 3.5%. Sac shrinkage >5mm at 5 years: 64%. Device migration: 2.1%
landmarkTrials.impact: Provided contemporary, real-world data on EVAR outcomes with modern devices. Five-year EVAR outcomes are excellent with low aneurysm-related mortality (5%). Sac shrinkage and low endoleak rates represent a significant improvement over earlier generations of EVAR devices
landmarkTrials.reference: Rhee RY, et al. PIVOTAL Trial 5-Year Results. J Vasc Surg. 2019;69(5):1399-1406

landmarkTrials.trial: RESCAN Collaborators (Meta-analysis of Small AAA Surveillance Data)
landmarkTrials.population: Individual patient data from 15,475 patients with small AAAs from 18 studies — largest pooled analysis to date
landmarkTrials.finding: Women with AAA rupture at 2-3x the rate of men at the same diameter. Smoking doubles the expansion rate. Expansion rate: 2.2 mm/year for 3.0-4.0 cm, 3.0 mm/year for 4.0-5.5 cm. Diabetes reduces expansion rate by 30% (paradoxical protective effect). Mean time from 4.0 cm to 5.5 cm: 6 years
landmarkTrials.impact: Validated different surveillance intervals for men vs women. Women should be offered repair at smaller diameters (5.0 cm vs 5.5 cm men). Smoking cessation is the only modifiable intervention that slows expansion. Diabetic patients can have less frequent surveillance

evidenceTable.recommendation: One-time abdominal ultrasound screening for AAA in all men aged 65-74 years
evidenceTable.reference: MASS Trial; Thomson SG, Lancet 2007; US Preventative Services Task Force Grade B

evidenceTable.recommendation: Surveillance ultrasound for AAA 3.0-3.9 cm q3 years; 4.0-4.4 cm q12-18 months; 4.5-5.4 cm q6-12 months
Elective AAA repair is indicated when AAA diameter ≥5.5 cm in men (≥5.0 cm in women) OR expansion rate >1 cm/year OR symptomatic
evidenceTable.reference: UKSAT, Lancet 1998; ADAM Trial, Lederle FA, NEJM 2002

evidenceTable.recommendation: EVAR is preferred for suitable anatomy (proximal neck ≥15 mm, angle <60°, iliac landing zone ≥20 mm) in patients at high operative risk
evidenceTable.reference: EVAR-1 Trial, Lancet 2005; DREAM Trial, NEJM 2005

evidenceTable.recommendation: Open surgical repair is preferred for young (<70 years), low-risk patients, or if EVAR anatomy is unsuitable
Smoking cessation reduces AAA expansion rate by 30-40% and should be strongly advised in all AAA patients
evidenceTable.reference: Brady AR, et al. BMJ 2004; Teng B, et al. JVS 2021

evidenceTable.recommendation: Ruptured AAA: emergency repair (EVAR or open) within 2 hours of diagnosis. Permissive hypotension (SBP 70-90 mmHg) before aortic control
Lifelong CT surveillance after EVAR: at 1 month, 6 months, 12 months, then annually. Imaging for endoleak, sac growth, device migration
Statins and antiplatelet therapy (aspirin 75-100 mg daily) should be prescribed in all AAA patients for cardiovascular risk reduction
Beta-blockers are NOT recommended routinely for AAA patients (no evidence of reduced expansion or rupture), but should be used for perioperative cardiac risk reduction in open repair
EVAR is NOT recommended for patients who are unfit for open repair due to limited life expectancy from comorbidities (no survival benefit per EVAR-2)
Women should be considered for elective AAA repair at ≥5.0 cm diameter (lower threshold than men) due to 2-3x higher rupture risk at equivalent size
First-degree relatives of AAA patients (men >50, women >55) should undergo one-time ultrasound screening for AAA
evidenceTable.reference: ESVS Guidelines; MASS Trial; UK NAAA Screening Program

evidenceTable.recommendation: Endoleak Type I or III after EVAR requires urgent reintervention (balloon-expandable stent, cuff extension, or surgical conversion). Type II endoleak with sac growth >5mm should be considered for embolization
Preoperative cardiac evaluation with echocardiography and stress testing is recommended before elective AAA repair in patients with known CAD or significant risk factors

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm: Incidental finding (abdominal ultrasound, CT, MRI) OR symptomatic (back pain, pulsatile mass, shock) → Confirm with CT angiogram (non-urgent) or FAST (if ruptured) → Assess: AAA diameter, morphology (saccular vs fusiform), proximal neck length and angle, iliac involvement, ILT, thrombus → RUPTURED? → Yes: permissive hypotension (SBP 70-90), reverse Trendelenburg, cross-match 8 units, NOTIFY OR. Assess hemodynamic instability. Stable? → CTA (if time permits) → anatomical suitability for EVAR vs open. Unstable? → Go directly to OR: open repair with infrarenal cross-clamp. → NOT RUPTURED (elective): → Asymptomatic (screening/diagnostic): size <5.5 cm (men) or <5.0 cm (women) → surveillance program. Size ≥5.5 cm (men) or ≥5.0 cm (women) → evaluate for elective repair. Symptomatic (pain, tenderness) → consider repair regardless of size. → Pre-operative assessment: cardiac risk (METs >4, no unstable CAD → proceed). Renal function, pulmonary function (especially for open). CTA for EVAR planning: neck length ≥15mm, neck angle <60°, iliac diameter 7-25mm, no circumferential thrombus at neck. → Decision: EVAR (preferred for suitable anatomy in high-risk patients). Open (preferred for young/low-risk patients or unsuitable EVAR anatomy). → Post-repair: EVAR: CT at 1, 6, 12 months then annual → screen for endoleak, sac growth, device migration. Open: clinical follow-up at 1, 6, 12 months then annual ● In Kenya: screening is not routine. Most AAAs are diagnosed only after rupture. Limited EVAR availability (cost, training, stock) means most repairs in Kenya are open. Pre-operative cardiopulmonary optimization is limited — zebra anaesthetic management is key.

managementPearls.context: Size matters, but so does sex
managementPearls.pearl: AAA criteria are sex-specific: ≥3.0 cm in men, ≥2.8 cm in women. But women rupture at smaller sizes: a 5.0 cm AAA in a woman has the same rupture risk as a 5.5 cm AAA in a man. Therefore, women should be offered repair at 5.0 cm (some guidelines say 4.5-5.0). Female sex is an independent risk factor for rupture (RR 3.0).
managementPearls.evidence: ESVS Guidelines; UKSAT substudy, Brown LC, BMJ 2003

managementPearls.pearl: A patient with a 4.0 cm AAA but with abdominal or back pain may be symptomatic from the AAA. Symptomatic AAA (tender aneurysm, pain not explained by other causes) has a 30-50% 1-year rupture risk — regardless of size. This is a surgical emergency. Never discharge a patient with a tender AAA awaiting surveillance — admit for urgent repair.

Inflammatory AAA presents with back pain, weight loss, and significantly elevated ESR/CRP — easily mistaken for TB, malignancy, or aortitis. CT shows a thick-walled aneurysm with adjacent fibrosis. Steroids (prednisolone 30-60 mg/day) can reduce inflammation but do not reduce rupture risk. These AAAs still need repair — but the tissue planes are difficult, and open repair has higher morbidity.
managementPearls.evidence: Rasmussen TE, et al. J Vasc Surg 2007; ESVS Guidelines

managementPearls.pearl: Type I: leak at proximal (Ia) or distal (Ib) attachment site — requires urgent treatment (balloon-expandable stent, cuff, or conversion). Type II: retrograde flow from lumbar/inferior mesenteric artery — serial observation if sac stable; embolize if sac grows >5mm. Type III: device fabric tear/junctional separation — urgent relining. Type IV: graft porosity — self-limited. Endotension (Type V): sac pressurization without visible leak — controversial management.
managementPearls.evidence: ESVS Guidelines; Chaikof EL, J Vasc Surg 2002

managementPearls.pearl: Smoking accelerates AAA expansion by 30-50% (average 0.3 mm/year vs 0.2 mm/year in non-smokers). Smoking cessation reduces the expansion rate to that of never-smokers within 1 year. One of the few interventions that truly modifies AAA biology. Every clinic visit should include smoking cessation counseling for AAA patients.
managementPearls.evidence: Brady AR, et al. BMJ 2004; MacSweeney ST, et al. Br J Surg 1994

managementPearls.context: The 5 cm threshold is not absolute
managementPearls.pearl: Consider repair below 5.5 cm in: women (5.0 cm threshold), saccular AAA (higher rupture risk regardless of size), rapid expansion (>1 cm/year or >5 mm in 6 months), concomitant iliac aneurysm (>3.5 cm), family history of ruptured AAA (first-degree relative), or low surgical risk patient who prefers repair. Always individualize.

managementPearls.context: The 'fit for surgery' assessment — more than just age
managementPearls.pearl: Age alone should NOT determine candidacy for AAA repair. A fit 80-year-old may have lower perioperative risk than an unfit 60-year-old. Use objective tools: (1) Duke Activity Status Index (DASI) — ask 'can you walk up two flights of stairs without stopping?' If yes, METs >4 — safe for surgery. (2) Frailty assessment: gait speed <0.8 m/s, unintentional weight loss, exhaustion — frailty predicts 2x mortality independent of age. (3) CPET (cardiopulmonary exercise testing): anaerobic threshold <11 mL/kg/min predicts high risk. (4) Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI): 0-2 factors = low risk; 3+ = high risk. In Kenya, CPET is rarely available, but DASI and frailty assessments are free and should guide decision-making.
managementPearls.evidence: Poon JT, et al. Frailty and AAA. JVS 2020; DASI, Hlatky MA, Am J Cardiol 1989

managementPearls.pearl: The old practice of aggressive fluid resuscitation in ruptured AAA (driving the BP to 'normal') increases bleeding, dilutes coagulation factors, and worsens outcomes. Permissive hypotension (SBP 70-90 mmHg for 30-60 minutes until aortic control) reduces mortality. The physiology: the retroperitoneum tamponades the rupture. Raising BP disrupts this tamponade, rebleeds are often fatal. Give only enough fluid to maintain consciousness. Use whole blood or 1:1:1 ratio (PRBC : FFP : Platelets). Avoid crystalloid boluses — dilute clotting factors. Target SBP 70-90 until cross-clamp. At clamp: you can then resuscitate to normal BP. In Kenya: permissive hypotension is even more critical where blood products are scarce.
managementPearls.evidence: IMPROVE Trial, Lancet 2014; ESVS Guidelines; Roberts K, et al. Massive Transfusion. Br J Surg 2018

managementPearls.context: EVAR surveillance — what to look for and when to act
managementPearls.pearl: The most common cause of late EVAR failure is sac growth WITHOUT a visible endoleak (endotension). If sac grows >5 mm on CT at 1 month, 6 months, or annually, do the following: (1) Check for Type I/III endoleak on CTA — if present, urgent relining or repair. (2) If no clear endoleak: perform Duplex with contrast (microbubbles) — more sensitive for slow-flow endoleaks. (3) If still no leak: measure sac pressure (sac puncture or implantable pressure sensor if used). (4) If sac continues to grow without identified cause: consider open conversion. The key rule: any sac growth >5 mm on two consecutive studies warrants aggressive investigation and likely intervention. Do NOT just 'watch and wait' with a growing sac — rupture risk is real.
managementPearls.evidence: Chaikof EL, JVS 2002; ESVS Guidelines; Veith FJ, et al. Sac enlargement. JVS 2017

managementPearls.context: Statin therapy — the forgotten aneurysm drug
managementPearls.pearl: Statins are not just for cholesterol — they have 'pleiotropic' effects on AAA: (1) reduce MMP-9 and MMP-2 expression in the aortic wall (direct anti-proteolytic effect); (2) reduce inflammation (decrease CRP, IL-6, TNF-α); (3) improve endothelial function (upregulate eNOS); (4) reduce thrombus formation within the aneurysm sac. Observational data: atorvastatin 80 mg daily reduces AAA expansion rate by 30% (0.25 vs 0.35 cm/year). The effect is independent of cholesterol lowering. Every AAA patient should be on a high-dose statin regardless of their lipid profile — the CV risk reduction and anti-aneurysm effect are both significant.
managementPearls.evidence: Heart Protection Study, Lancet 2002; Schouten O, et al. Statins and AAA expansion. Eur J Vasc Endovasc Surg 2006; ESVS Guidelines

managementPearls.context: Preventing colonic ischemia after open repair
managementPearls.pearl: Colonic ischemia is a devastating complication of AAA repair (1-3% incidence, 50-80% mortality). Prevention: (1) Identify the IMA during dissection — if it is patent and there is no other source of left colon perfusion, reimplant it into the graft (Carrel patch). (2) Check for back-bleeding from the IMA after opening the sac — if there is vigorous back-bleeding, the IMA has good collateral flow and is safe to ligate. (3) After repair, assess colon viability: look at the sigmoid colon — is it pink? Does it have peristalsis? Doppler of colonic marginal artery. If any doubt, perform proctosigmoidoscopy. (4) Post-operative clues: bloody diarrhea, rising lactate, leukocytosis, fever — require urgent sigmoidoscopy. If ischemia detected: antibiotics, bowel rest, and surgical resection if transmural necrosis.
managementPearls.evidence: Björck M, et al. Colonic ischemia after AAA. Eur J Vasc Endovasc Surg 2010; ESVS Guidelines

operativeNuances.pitfall: Injury to the renal veins (especially the left renal vein that crosses the aorta) or the duodenum, which is adherent to the AAA sac
operativeNuances.expertTip: Approach the aorta through a midline laparotomy. Reflect the transverse colon superiority, small bowel to the right. Incise the posterior peritoneum to the left of the duodenum (Kocher maneuver). Identify the left renal vein crossing the aorta — it is your landmark for the infrarenal neck. The duodenum is often stuck to the AAA — do not dissect it off. Prepare the neck just below the renal veins (at least 1.5 cm of non-aneurysmal aorta). Give heparin (100 IU/kg) before cross-clamping. Cross-clamp at the infrarenal neck, then the iliac arteries. Open the sac longitudinally, oversew lumbar arteries from within the sac.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Inability to advance the delivery sheath through tortuous, calcified, or small (<6 mm) iliac arteries, leading to iliac rupture
operativeNuances.expertTip: Pre-operatively assess CTA for iliac morphology. If external iliac diameter <7 mm, consider an iliac conduit (suturing a 10 mm Dacron graft to the common iliac artery as a landing zone) or use a lower-profile device. If tortuous iliac, use a stiff wire (Lunderquist or Amplatz Super Stiff) and sequentially dilate with the sheath. If resistance is met, STOP, and perform an iliac angiogram to exclude perforation. Have covered stents ready for iliac rupture.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Large midline incision with significant fluid loss, hypothermia, and prolonged ileus in elderly patients
operativeNuances.expertTip: In thin patients, consider the 'mini-laparotomy' approach: a 10-12 cm incision from the xiphoid to the umbilicus. Use a hand-held retractor. This is sufficient for infrarenal AAA. Avoids the large incision. Not suitable for obese patients, large AAAs (>7 cm), or concomitant iliac aneurysm requiring distal exposure. The technique reduces ICU stay by 1-2 days without increasing morbidity.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Uncontrolled hemorrhage while trying to achieve proximal control in a hostile, bloody surgical field
operativeNuances.expertTip: For unstable ruptured AAA: use a compliant CODA balloon (or a Fogarty catheter) placed via percutaneous femoral access (12F sheath) and inflated in the supraceliac aorta. This provides immediate proximal control while you prepare for open repair. Deflate and reposition to infrarenal after dissection. The 'endoclamp' technique has been a game-changer for ruptured AAA — it converts an emergency case into an elective dissection.

operativeNuances.procedure: Suprapubic (Transverse) Incision vs Midline for Open AAA Repair
operativeNuances.pitfall: Midline laparotomy has higher rates of incisional hernia (10-15%), longer ileus, and more postoperative respiratory complications compared to a transverse incision
operativeNuances.expertTip: For elective infrarenal AAA repair in thin-to-moderate BMI patients, consider a transverse supraumbilical ('smile') incision from mid-axillary line to mid-axillary line. Advantages: (1) less pain (cuts across dermatomes — fewer muscle-splitting spasms); (2) lower hernia rate (3-5% vs 10-15% for midline); (3) earlier return of bowel function. Disadvantages: (1) more bleeding from rectus sheath vessels; (2) limited exposure of the right iliac system; (3) takes longer to open and close. My preference: transverse for thin women with small AAA (<6 cm), midline for obese, large AAA (>7 cm), or ruptured AAA (speed). In Kenya, where incisional hernias are a major problem (late presentation after repair, poor compliance with lifting restrictions), a transverse incision should be used whenever feasible.

operativeNuances.procedure: IMA Reimplantation (Carrel Patch) During Open AAA Repair
operativeNuances.pitfall: Ligating a patent IMA without assessing colonic collateral circulation → left colonic ischemia → 50-80% mortality
operativeNuances.expertTip: Decision to reimplant IMA: (1) Assess pre-operative CTA: is the IMA patent? Are there collaterals from the SMA (meandering mesenteric artery)? (2) Intra-operative: after opening the AAA sac, assess IMA back-bleeding. If there is NO back-bleeding from the IMA (suggests poor collateral flow from SMA), reimplantation is mandatory. If there is good back-bleeding, you can oversew the IMA. (3) Reimplantation technique: excise a 1 cm diameter Carrel patch of aortic wall containing the IMA orifice. Sew this onto the graft (proximal or distal body) using 4-0 or 5-0 Prolene. Test by removing the clamp from the left limb: the IMA patch should bleed vigorously. If it does not, it may be thrombosed. This simple technique (5 extra minutes) saves lives.

prognosis.mortality: Elective EVAR: 30-day mortality 1-2%. Elective open repair: 30-day mortality 3-5% (high-volume centers: 2-3%, low-volume: 5-8%). Ruptured AAA overall: 80% die before reaching hospital (pre-hospital mortality). In-hospital mortality for those reaching hospital: 40-50% (even in modern centers). EVAR for ruptured: 30-day mortality 30-35%. Open for ruptured: 40-50%. In Kenya, ruptured AAA mortality is likely 60-80% due to lack of pre-hospital care, limited blood products, and delayed presentation.
prognosis.morbidity: EVAR: endoleak 20-30% (Type II in 10-20%; Type I in 5-10%). Reintervention rate: 3-5% per year. Graft migration: 5% at 5 years. Open repair: cardiac complications 5-10% (MI, arrhythmia). Renal failure: 3-5% (higher if suprarenal clamp). Colon ischemia: 1-3% (check left colon after repair). Sexual dysfunction: 10-20% (injury to hypogastric plexus — avoid dissection over left common iliac artery). Incisional hernia: 10% (late). Graft infection: 0.5-1% (devastating — 50% mortality).
prognosis.survival: Elective repair (all): 5-year survival 70-80% (competing cardiovascular mortality — most die of other causes, not AAA). EVAR vs open: aneurysm-related mortality similar at 5 years (EVAR: 4%, open: 3%). Overall survival: equivalent. Ruptured AAA survivors: 5-year survival 60-70%. Young patients (<65) after elective repair: 10-year survival 60-70% (near-matched to population). Advanced age (>80) after elective repair: 4-year survival 50%.
prognosis.recurrence: After EVAR: 20-30% will require reintervention within 5 years (endoleak, limb occlusion, sac growth). Open repair: 0.5-1% anastomotic pseudoaneurysm at 10 years (higher if infection). Ruptured AAA survivors: 5% risk of secondary rupture at other sites (iliac, thoracic). Contralateral iliac aneurysm: 10-15% have >3 cm iliac aneurysm — needs surveillance. Overall: after EVAR, lifelong CT surveillance is mandatory.
prognosis.reference: EVAR-1, Lancet 2005; UKSAT, Lancet 1998; IMPROVE, Lancet 2014; ESVS Guidelines; ADAM Trial, NEJM 2002
